title: Arnold Conjecture Over Integers
speaker: Shaoyun Bai
abstract: We show that for any closed symplectic manifold, the number of 1-periodic orbits of any non-degenerate Hamiltonian is bounded from below by a version of total Betti number over Z, which takes account of torsions of all characteristics. The proof relies on an abstract perturbation scheme (FOP perturbations) which allows us to produce integral pseudo-cycles from moduli space of J-holomorphic curves, and a geometric regularization scheme for moduli space of Hamiltonian Floer trajectories generalizing the recent work of Abouzaid-McLean-Smith.

proof_stages.label: Homological Arnold lower bound with collapsed grading
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up nondegenerate Hamiltonian fixed points on a closed symplectic manifold, defines the minimal Chern number and the Z/2N-reduced integral homology invariant counting free rank plus twice the number of torsion factors, and states the integral Arnold lower bound together with comparisons and limitations.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to integral Floer complex and PSS injection
proof_stages.summary: The proof is reduced to constructing a Z/2N-graded Hamiltonian Floer complex over the integral Novikov ring, freely generated by fixed points, together with PSS and SSP maps whose Morse-side composition is chain homotopic to the identity plus positive-valuation terms, hence injective and sufficient for the lower bound.

proof_stages.label: Technical package for integer Floer counts
proof_stages.summary: The construction is said to rely on two main technical inputs: a perturbation theory extracting integral pseudocycle counts from normally complex derived orbifolds, including multiplicativity for products of boundary strata, and global Kuranishi/orbifold charts with smooth structures for Floer moduli spaces.

proof_stages.label: Normally complex derived orbifold charts
proof_stages.summary: A derived orbifold chart is formulated as a smooth orbifold, orbibundle, and section whose zero set models a holomorphic-curve moduli space, and normal complex structure means the nontrivial isotropy representations in both tangent and obstruction directions carry compatible complex structures.

proof_stages.label: FOP perturbations and multiplicative pseudocycles
proof_stages.summary: For a compact normally complex derived orbifold chart, a C0-small strongly transverse Fukaya-Oh-Parker perturbation has free zero locus defining an integral pseudocycle well-defined up to bordism, and the strong transversality condition is preserved under products, which is crucial for Floer differentials and compositions.

proof_stages.label: Normal-complex verification for holomorphic-curve moduli
proof_stages.summary: The speaker argues that in the relevant Gromov-Witten, Hamiltonian Floer, and Lagrangian Floer settings, nontrivial isotropy comes from multiply covered sphere components, whose noninvariant deformation complex is homotopic to a complex-linear Cauchy-Riemann operator, so regularized moduli spaces carry the normal-complex structure needed for integral counts.

examples.name: Z/2 in H7 and Z/3 in H9 collapsing to odd Z/6

examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the collapse of Z/2 in H7 and Z/3 in H9 under mod-2 grading, combines them as odd Z/6, and notes the resulting contribution to the lower bound

examples.name: Algebraic-variety closure analogy for complex polynomial perturbations
examples.note: In response to questions, the speaker explains that complex polynomial perturbations make zero loci behave like algebraic varieties whose added boundary has real codimension at least two, which is the intuition behind producing pseudocycles.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the algebraic-variety closure analogy to explain how complex polynomial perturbations give codimension-at-least-two boundary and hence a homology class

passes.reason: The complex-algebraic closure picture is a standard analogy for codimension-two boundary behavior under polynomial perturbations.
